Take the CAT

This Computerized Adaptive Test measures your position on economic policy ideology on a single progressive ↔ conservative dimension. After each answer, the test estimates your ideology and selects the next item to be maximally informative for you.

How to take the test
  1. Click Start Test button below.
  2. For each question, click option A or option B, whichever best reflects your view.
  3. The test stops automatically when measurement precision is sufficient (SE < 0.30) or after 20 items.
  4. Your final ideology estimate will appear at the end of the test.

See the Overview tab for the construct, item bank, methodology, and insights.

Demo Respondents

Purpose. These three walkthroughs illustrate how the CAT adapts to different ideology profiles. Responses at each step are drawn from the calibrated 2PL model. No real person takes the test; the simulated respondent answers probabilistically according to their true theta.

Specifications & what each panel shows
  • Progressive (true theta = -1.5): strongly progressive profile; the CAT should converge to a negative theta_hat.
  • Moderate (true theta = 0.0): centrist profile; typically reaches SE < 0.30 in the fewest items.
  • Conservative (true theta = +1.5): strongly conservative profile; the CAT should converge to a positive theta_hat.

Each demo's summary row lists true theta, final theta_hat, final SE, number of items administered, and stop reason. Use Prev / Next to step through each item and see the item stem, response, updated theta_hat and SE, ICC, IIC, and the selection justification.

Step 1

Adaptive vs. Linear Test: SE Trajectory

Both tests use the same item pool, the same calibrated parameters, and the same respondent's true theta; only the item selection policy differs. The adaptive curve (solid) picks the most informative remaining item at each step; the linear curve (dashed) administers items in fixed ID order. Gap between the two curves at any n quantifies the CAT's efficiency advantage.

Both tests use the same item pool, the same calibrated parameters, and the same respondent's true theta; only the item selection policy differs. The adaptive curve (solid) picks the most informative remaining item at each step; the linear curve (dashed) administers items in fixed ID order. Gap between the two curves at any n quantifies the CAT's efficiency advantage.

Economic Policy Ideology CAT

This report documents a complete Computerized Adaptive Test (CAT) pipeline built around a 30 item bank measuring a single latent dimension of economic policy ideology (progressive ↔ conservative). Items were generated with Claude assistance and span six subdomains: taxation, government spending, labor markets, healthcare, corporate regulation, and trade.

Why 2PL?

Each item presents two substantively grounded policy positions (option A = progressive, option B = conservative). Because neither option is "correct," there is no guessing parameter, making the 3PL model inefficient. The two-parameter logistic (2PL) model captures variation in both the location (ideological threshold, parameter b) and slope (discriminating power, parameter a) of each item's characteristic curve.

Target population

English-speaking adults with an opinion on national economic policy. The latent trait theta is standardized so that theta = 0 corresponds to the ideological center; negative values indicate progressive orientation and positive values indicate conservative orientation.

Item Bank

The pool contains 30 item questions across six subdomains. Calibrated discrimination (a) and difficulty (b) parameters, and the item-total correlation (ITC), are shown below. Items are sortable by clicking the column headers. Rows marked with a red asterisk contribute weakly to measurement (a < 0.7 or ITC < 0.20) and would be candidates for revision or removal in a production bank.

Data Simulation

Five hundred respondents' theta values were drawn from a t(df=10) distribution rescaled to unit variance, heavier tails than N(0, 1) so that items with |b| > 1.2 receive adequate coverage. For each person-item pair, the 2PL model determined P(B=1 | theta), and a binary response was Bernoulli-sampled from that probability (not a uniform coin flip), satisfying the "not fully random" hint in the project details. True item parameters were based on content review of each item's disc_label (High / Moderate / Low) and its ideological direction.

Calibration

Item parameters were estimated via Marginal Maximum Likelihood EM using the girth library (twopl_mml). The algorithm marginalizes over theta using Gauss-Hermite quadrature and iterates until convergence. The plots below compare the generating ("true") parameters to the MML-recovered estimates.

Important interpretation note. Because the simulated responses were produced from the exact 2PL model that is then re-fit during calibration, high recovery correlations demonstrate simulation fidelity (the estimator can invert the generating model) rather than empirical validity (items behaving as 2PL on real human responses).

0.964
Pearson r (a)
0.993
Pearson r (b)
0.164
RMSE (a)
0.075
RMSE (b)

Theta recovery

Each respondent's true theta (used to generate their responses) is compared against their Expected A Posteriori estimate computed from the calibrated item parameters. A high Pearson r and tight scatter around the identity line indicate that the 30-item pool can reliably recover a respondent's latent ideology from their binary responses alone.

Reliability and Validity

Cronbach alpha
0.839
Marginal reliability
6.428
1st eigenvalue
1.221
2nd eigenvalue

Unidimensionality

The scree plot below shows eigenvalues of the inter-item Pearson correlation matrix. Clear dominance of the first eigenvalue supports the single-factor assumption required by 2PL.

Test Information Function

The TIF shows where along the ideology continuum the test is most informative. Peak information near theta = 0 reflects the balanced item bank design.

Item Fit (Infit Mean-Square)

Infit MSQ is the information weighted mean-square residual per item: MSQj = Σi (xij - Pij)2 / Σi Pij(1 - Pij). Its expected value under correct 2PL fit is 1.0. Values below 0.7 suggest the item is over-predictable (redundant with others), while values above 1.0 suggest noise or multidimensionality.

Item Catalogue and Subscale Structure

All 30 Item Characteristic Curves

Each panel plots one item's probability of a conservative (B) response as a function of theta. The solid curve uses the true (generating) parameters; the dashed curve uses the calibrated estimates.

Subscale Test Information Functions

Each subscale's five items produce their own TIF. Plotting the six curves together reveals where (on the theta continuum) each policy subdomain carries the most measurement precision. Together they cover the full theta range reasonably evenly, which is what a general-purpose economic ideology test is expected to be.

Inter-Subscale Correlations

Pearson correlations between the six subscale sum scores (each 0-5). Strong positive correlations indicate a shared underlying factor (support for the unidimensional 2PL model); very high values (r > 0.85) would suggest subscale redundancy.

CAT Engine Design

Start rule

Begin with the item that maximizes Fisher information at theta = 0 (the prior mean). This is the same objective used for every subsequent step, the start rule is the step-wise max-information rule evaluated with the initial theta estimate. The program picks the item with the largest a2·P(0)·(1 - P(0)) across the item bank .csv file.

Item selection

At each step, the program uses the Fisher information I(theta_hat) = a2·P(theta_hat)·(1 - P(theta_hat)) for every unadministered item and select the item with the highest value. Each item may be administered at most once per respondent.

Theta estimation

Theta is estimated by maximum likelihood using the Brent's method. When all responses so far are identical (e.g., all B or all A), MLE diverges; in that case, Expected A Posteriori estimation with a N(0, 1) prior is used as a fallback. The standard error is SE = 1 / sqrt(sum of item Fisher information at theta_hat).

Stop rule

Testing stops when SE < 0.30 (measurement precision achieved) or when 20 items have been administered (maximum length), whichever comes first.

Conclusion

Limitations

  • Circular validation by construction. Responses were generated from the 2PL model and then fit with the same 2PL model. The high parameter-recovery correlations (r > 0.95 for both a and b) should be read as simulation-fidelity evidence, not as evidence that the items behave as 2PL on real human respondents.
  • True parameters. The "true" a and b values were assigned by content review of each item (informed priors, not empirical values). The disc_label column (High / Moderate / Low) is metadata that guided authoring, not a calibrated discrimination.
  • Pool depth. The CAT draws from 30 items with a max test length of 20, giving a 1.5:1 pool-to-length ratio. Operational CATs typically deploy with 5-10:1 ratios. This cap is why all three demo respondents administer all 20 items without crossing the SE < 0.30 stop threshold. The Adaptive vs. Linear trajectory plots show the CAT still gains precision on every item, but the target is not reachable within the cap.
  • Three low-discrimination items. Items 19 (Rural broadband access), 20 (Public library expansion), and 30 (Trade-displaced worker aid) have atrue ≤ 0.6 and item-total r < 0.20. They contribute minimal Fisher information and rarely appear in adaptive sessions. In a production bank they would be flagged for revision or removal.
  • Target population is asserted, not piloted. The construct and the intended audience are theoretical framings. No human pilot has been administered, so construct and content validity remain argued.
  • Single dimension. Economic ideology is multidimensional in practice (e.g., fiscal vs. regulatory preferences). The first-eigenvalue ratio supports the unidimensional 2PL assumption for this simulated data, but the same analysis on real responses could reveal additional factors.
  • Theta draw shape. Respondents' theta was drawn from a t(df=10) distribution rescaled to unit variance to improve tail coverage. The mismatch is small and does not visibly perturb recovery, but a full demonstration would use N(0, 1).
  • Binary forced choice. This format eliminates biases but limits each item to one bit of information and may frustrate respondents holding nuanced positions.
  • MLE instability on uniform responses. Early in the CAT with all-same responses, the MLE diverges. An EAP fallback with an N(0, 1) prior keeps the theta estimate bounded but introduces prior dependence until mixed responses are observed.

Challenges encountered

  • Selecting an item-fit statistic that is both interpretable and correct. Averaging raw standardized residuals produces values near zero for any item (positive and negative residuals cancel), so we moved to the information-weighted mean-square (infit MSQ) with the standard 0.7-1.3 band.
  • Packaging the entire pipeline output into a single self-contained HTML meant inlining every Plotly figure, the full calibrated parameter table, and precomputed demo traces, making the file > 1 MB but fully portable and server-less.
  • Demonstrating "adaptive" behavior visibly when the SE target is not reachable within the pool cap: the Adaptive vs. Linear trajectory plot was added so the efficiency advantage can be visible..

Suggested improvements

  • Expand the bank to 60-120 items, concentrating new items at |theta| > 1 so extreme respondents can reach SE < 0.30 within the cap.
  • Retire or rewrite items 19, 20, and 30 on the basis of low a and low item-total r.
  • Run a small human pilot to obtain real responses and refit the 2PL. Report recovery against the pilot-calibrated parameters as a validity check.
  • Consider exposure control (e.g., Sympson-Hetter) so high-information items at theta ~ 0 are not repeatedly selected in large-scale administration.
